author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
On the Ignorance of Infants, and Whence It Arises.
title: On the Ignorance of Infants, and Whence It Arises.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Chapter 67 [XXXVI.]—On the Ignorance of Infants, and Whence It Arises.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Yes, let us consider that darkness of their rational intellect, by reason of which they are even completely ignorant of God, whose sacraments they actually struggle against, while being baptized.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Now my inquiry is, When and whence came they to be immersed in this darkness?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Is it then the fact that they incurred it all here, and in this their own proper life forgat God through too much negligence, after a life of wisdom and religion in their mother’s womb?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Let those say so who dare; let them listen to it who wish to; let them believe it who can.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: I, however, am sure that none whose minds are not blinded by an obstinate adherence to a foregone conclusion can possibly entertain such an opinion.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Is there then no evil in ignorance,—nothing which needs to be purged away?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: What means that prayer “Remember not the sins of my youth and of my ignorance?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: For although those sins are more to be condemned which are knowingly committed, yet if there were no sins of ignorance, we should not have read in Scripture what I have quoted, “Remember not the sins of my youth and of my ignorance.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Seeing now that the soul of an infant fresh from its mother’s womb is still the soul of a human being,—nay, the soul of a rational creature,—not only untaught, but even incapable of instruction, I ask why, or when, or whence, it was plunged into that thick darkness of ignorance in which it lies?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: If it is man’s nature thus to begin, and that nature is not already corrupt, then why was not Adam created thus?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Why was he capable of receiving a commandment? and able to give names to his wife, and to all the animal creation?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: For of her he said, “She shall be called Woman;” and in respect of the rest we read: “Whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Whereas this one, although he is ignorant where he is, what he is, by whom created, of what parents born, is already guilty of offence, incapable as yet of receiving a commandment, and so completely involved and overwhelmed in a thick cloud of ignorance, that he cannot be aroused out of his sleep, so as to recognize even these facts; but a time must be patiently awaited, until he can shake off this strange intoxication, as it were, (not indeed in a single night, as even the heaviest drunkenness usually can be, but) little by little, through many months, and even years; and until this be accomplished, we have to bear in little children so many things which we punish in older persons, that we cannot enumerate them.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins, and on the Baptism of Infants.
verses.text: Now, as touching this enormous evil of ignorance and weakness, if in this present life infants have contracted it as soon as they were born, where, when, how, have they by the perpetration of some great iniquity become suddenly implicated in such darkness?
